rationale: The citing paper does more than list this source; it explicitly summarizes Chevalier and Mayzlin (2006) as evidence that online reviews affect sales and that low ratings can have stronger effects than high ratings. That prior result is used to motivate why negative bias in reviews without confirmed purchases is managerially important. The paper also cites it again when interpreting its own revenue effects as consistent with prior review-sales evidence, giving the citation a clear framing and interpretive role.
relation_description: The citing paper uses Chevalier and Mayzlin (2006) as an empirical foundation that review valence, especially negative ratings, can materially affect demand. It builds its motivation and interpretation of sales consequences of distorted low ratings on that prior evidence.
